### Applications per Credential

The average number of distinct applications a single credential signs in to.

This metric measures how far the typical user spreads across CanadaLogin-integrated
applications. It works because per-application `unique_users` counts each person
once *per application*, while the CanadaLogin-wide count counts each person once
overall: a user active on three applications adds three to the per-application
sum but only one to the overall total. Dividing the two recovers the average
number of applications per credential.

A value of 1.0 means the typical credential touches exactly one application;
2.0 means two, and so on.

*Alternative names*:

- average applications per user
- application breadth per credential
- multi-app usage
- cross-application reach
- apps per account

**Calculation.** Sum the latest year-to-date unique users across every
application (from `app_login_counts`), then divide by the latest CanadaLogin-wide
year-to-date unique users (from `auth_total_logins`). Both inputs are cumulative
`ytd_unique_users` fields, so take the latest value per source rather than summing
across days.

::: {.panel-tabset}

## SQL

```sql
WITH app_latest AS (
    SELECT
        application_name,
        ytd_unique_users,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY application_name
            ORDER BY from_date DESC
        ) AS rn
    FROM ibm_verify.app_login_counts
),

app_total AS (
    SELECT SUM(ytd_unique_users) AS sum_app_unique_users
    FROM app_latest
    WHERE rn = 1
),

overall_total AS (
    SELECT ytd_unique_users AS overall_unique_users
    FROM ibm_verify.auth_total_logins
    ORDER BY from_date DESC
    LIMIT 1
)

SELECT
    CAST(app_total.sum_app_unique_users AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(overall_total.overall_unique_users, 0) AS applications_per_credential
FROM app_total
CROSS JOIN overall_total;
```

## R

```r
sum_app_unique_users <- tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "app_login_counts")) |>
  group_by(application_name) |>
  slice_max(
    from_date,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  summarise(
    sum_app_unique_users = sum(as.double(ytd_unique_users), na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  pull(sum_app_unique_users)

overall_unique_users <- tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  slice_max(
    from_date,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  pull(ytd_unique_users)

sum_app_unique_users / overall_unique_users
```

:::

*Interpretation notes.* This is a population average, not a per-user count: a
small group of power users spanning many applications can pull it up while most
credentials stay on one.

The figure is inflated by internal applications, especially the migration app
(`GCS Migration Solution`) and the profile management app
(`GC Sign In - Profile Management App`). A user signing in to a single public
service is still recorded as a unique user in the migration app when their
credential is migrated from ECM, and in the profile management app when they
manage their account, so they count two or three times in the per-application sum
while counting once overall. To measure breadth across public-facing services,
exclude the internal applications (listed at the top of this chapter) from the
per-application sum. 

Swap `ytd_unique_users` for `mtd_unique_users` or `since_apr22_unique_users` to
measure the same breadth over a different window.
